skill.name: agent-development
skill.description: Create, validate, and refine Claude Code agent definitions with guidance for frontmatter, triggering examples, tool permissions, and system prompts.
skill.summary: Build well-structured Claude Code agents with validated metadata, prompts, and trigger examples.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are primarily Markdown examples, fenced templates, and instructional prompt text. The bundled Bash validator uses local filesystem and text-processing commands on a user-provided file path, with no evidence of network access, credential theft, destructive behavior, or prompt-injection instructions targeting the evaluator.

low_findings.title: User-Invoked Shell Validator Reads Local Files
low_findings.description: The skill includes a Bash validator that runs local text-processing commands against the path supplied by the user. The path is quoted and the script performs validation output only, so this is a low-risk utility rather than command injection or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell behavior is directly visible and uses standard local commands such as head, tail, sed, awk, and grep. I found no unquoted shell interpolation, network call, or secret collection in the reviewed script.

low_findings.title: Static Command Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command findings occur inside fenced Markdown examples that teach agent structure, validation, and prompt design. These examples are not executed by the skill runtime and do not establish a command execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are fenced Markdown, JSON, YAML, or example text rather than executable source. The surrounding context describes how users should create agent files.

low_findings.title: Prompt-Like Text Is Reference Material
low_findings.description: The skill contains example system prompts with phrases such as "You are" because it teaches agent prompt design. I found no instruction to bypass security analysis, ignore evaluator instructions, or claim pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Prompt-like language is expected for this skill category and is consistently framed as examples for generated agents. Targeted search found no evaluator-directed override or skip-review language.

content.value_statement: Creating reliable AI agents is hard when triggers, tools, and system prompts are inconsistent. This skill provides templates, examples, and validation guidance for Claude Code agent development.
actual_capabilities: Explains required agent frontmatter fields, including name, description, model, color, and tools.
Provides templates for writing agent triggering examples with context, user messages, and commentary.
Shows complete example agents for code review, test generation, documentation, and security analysis.
Guides system prompt design with responsibilities, process steps, quality standards, and output formats.
Includes a shell validator for checking local agent Markdown file structure.
Recommends least-privilege tool selection for different agent types.
limitations: The validator checks file structure and simple text rules, not full semantic quality.
The examples target Claude Code plugin agents and may need adaptation for other platforms.
Generated agents still require human review before use in sensitive workflows.
The skill does not install agents or test live agent routing automatically.

use_cases.title: Create a New Plugin Agent
use_cases.description: Design an agent file with valid frontmatter, clear trigger examples, and a structured system prompt.

use_cases.title: Improve Agent Triggering
use_cases.description: Rewrite weak descriptions into concrete examples that show when an agent should be used.

use_cases.title: Validate Agent Files
use_cases.description: Check local agent Markdown files for required metadata, prompt presence, and recommended structure.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new agent skeleton.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code agent for [task]. Include the agent name, description, model, color, tool list, and a concise system prompt.

prompt_templates.title: Add Trigger Examples
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve an agent description so routing is more reliable.
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this agent description with three triggering examples. Include explicit, implicit, and proactive usage scenarios: [description].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Domain Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a complete specialist agent for a real workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an agent for [domain workflow]. Define responsibilities, process steps, quality standards, edge cases, and expected output format.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Agent Suite
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of multiple agents in a plugin.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these agent definitions for naming, trigger clarity, tool permissions, prompt completeness, and overlap. Recommend concrete improvements.

output_examples.input: Create an agent that reviews pull requests for code quality and security issues.
output_examples.output: A complete agent specification with a concise reviewer name, review-focused trigger examples, read-only tools, and a structured review process.

output_examples.input: My test generator agent does not trigger after I write code.
output_examples.output: A revised description with proactive examples that show code creation followed by test generation, plus clearer commentary for the trigger decision.

output_examples.input: Validate this agent file before I publish my plugin.
output_examples.output: A validation checklist covering frontmatter fields, prompt length, example blocks, tool permissions, and recommended fixes.

best_practices: Use specific trigger examples that match real user requests and agent handoffs.
Limit each agent to the minimum tools needed for its job.
Define responsibilities, process steps, quality standards, output format, and edge cases.
anti_patterns: Do not use generic agent names such as helper, assistant, or agent.
Do not omit examples from the description field when reliable triggering matters.
Do not grant Bash or write access to agents that only need read-only analysis.

faq.question: What kind of agents does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code plugin agents for review, generation, validation, documentation, and similar development workflows.

faq.question: Does this skill generate agents automatically?
faq.answer: It provides prompts, templates, examples, and validation guidance. The user still reviews and creates the final agent file.

faq.question: Can I use it with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is useful in Codex when you are authoring Claude Code compatible agent files or reviewing agent prompts.

faq.question: Why are triggering examples important?
faq.answer: Examples show when the agent should run and help the host model choose the right agent for a task.

faq.question: Does the validator execute agent code?
faq.answer: No. The included script reads a local Markdown file and checks structure, metadata, examples, and prompt text.

faq.question: Should every agent have Bash access?
faq.answer: No. The skill recommends least-privilege tool access, especially for review or analysis agents.
